does: This paper introduces two topology-informed alignment methods that enhance LLM training by regularizing the geometric structure of hidden representations. For supervised fine-tuning, Trajectory Topology Loss (TTL) uses persistent homology to identify topological connections between prompt and answer embeddings, then aligns the model's gradient updates toward these semantic bridges rather than arbitrary directions. For preference-based learning, Topological Preference Optimization (TPO) constructs topic-specific semantic preference vectors in intermediate hidden layers and steers the improvement direction between rejected and chosen responses to align with these vectors. A dynamic weighting scheme balances the two losses. The framework shifts from local token-likelihood or scalar-score optimization to leveraging global geometric structure of the representation space during training.
connects: This work extends the line of alignment research that includes SFT and RLHF/DPO methods, responding to a key limitation in these approaches: they operate on local signals (token probabilities, reward scores) without considering global semantic geometry. It builds on recent interest in understanding LLM representations through geometric and topological lens, combining ideas from persistent homology (a tool from computational topology) with modern alignment techniques. The work relates to broader efforts examining representation geometry during training and generation, and connects to preference optimization methods like DPO that compare model outputs. It addresses the gap between simple loss functions and the complex, high-dimensional structure of semantic space that generative trajectories actually traverse.
enables: This topology-enhanced framework opens directions for more geometrically-aware LLM training beyond alignment. Future work could apply persistent homology to other training objectives, explore how different topological properties of trajectories correlate with generation quality or interpretability, and investigate whether explicit geometric regularization improves robustness or out-of-distribution generalization. The method suggests that many LLM training problems might benefit from incorporating tools from topological data analysis rather than treating representations as unstructured. It also raises questions about what topological structures emerge naturally during generation and whether they can be directly optimized. Application to multimodal models, longer-horizon reasoning tasks, or foundation model pre-training represents potential extensions of this geometric alignment perspective.
